description: Movement of goods from origin to destination across legs, modes, and carriers. Levers are service quality (on-time, in-full, low damage), speed (transit and dwell time), and cost/asset efficiency (cost per shipment/mile, vehicle and container load factor, routes per driver).

metrics.name: On-Time Delivery Rate (OTD)
metrics.definition: Share of deliveries completed on or before the promised/committed time.
metrics.formula: COUNT(DISTINCT shipment_id WHERE actual_delivery_time <= promised_delivery_time) / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT delivered_shipment_id), 0)
metrics.grain: Shipment/delivery grain. Compare actual vs promised at the SAME entity. Denominator = shipments that were actually delivered (or due) in the window, not all created shipments. A rate is bounded 0..1 BY CONSTRUCTION.
anti_patterns: Counting tracking-event rows or legs instead of distinct shipments fans out the numerator → a rate >1, which means a join fan-out, not great service.
Denominator = all shipments (including in-transit/not-yet-due) understates OTD; restrict to delivered or past-promise shipments.
Comparing actual delivery time against carrier pickup time or order time instead of the PROMISED/committed delivery time measures the wrong SLA.

metrics.name: OTIF (On-Time In-Full)
metrics.definition: Share of orders delivered BOTH on time AND complete (full ordered quantity, no shortages).
metrics.formula: COUNT(DISTINCT order_id WHERE on_time = TRUE AND in_full = TRUE) / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT eligible_order_id), 0)
metrics.grain: Order grain — both conditions evaluated on the SAME order population. 'In full' is per-order: ALL lines delivered at ordered qty (use a per-order aggregate like MIN(delivered_qty >= ordered_qty) over lines, not a line-level rate). Do NOT compute on-time on one population and in-full on another and multiply the two rates.
anti_patterns: Computing OTD% and in-full% on different row sets then multiplying them — OTIF is a conjunction at the order grain, not a product of two independent marginals.
Evaluating in-full at the line grain (a 4-of-5-lines order counts as 80% full) instead of collapsing to a boolean per order first.
Denominator population differs from OTD's — keep the eligible-order set identical so OTIF <= OTD always holds.
metrics.sane_range: ratio 0..1; always <= On-Time Delivery Rate. Typical retail-supplier targets 0.95+.

metrics.name: Average Transit Time
metrics.definition: Average elapsed time from dispatch/pickup to final delivery.
metrics.formula: SUM(delivery_time - pickup_time) / COUNT(DISTINCT shipment_id)  -- end-to-end per shipment
metrics.grain: Shipment grain for end-to-end transit. If a shipment has multiple legs and you want a per-leg or modal average, DISTANCE-WEIGHT it: SUM(leg_duration) / SUM(leg_distance) gives time-per-distance; a plain AVG(leg_duration) over legs lets a 5-mile cross-dock hop count the same as a 2,000-mile linehaul.
anti_patterns: AVG over leg rows instead of per-shipment end-to-end double-counts multi-leg shipments and biases toward shipments with more legs.
Unweighted average of per-leg times when you actually want network speed — weight by distance (or leg count consistently), never mix.
Including dwell/idle hold time silently inflates transit; decide explicitly whether stops count and apply it uniformly.
metrics.sane_range: hours or days, strictly positive; last-mile parcel hours-to-days, ocean freight days-to-weeks. Negative or zero ⇒ swapped timestamps.

metrics.name: Cost per Shipment / Cost per Mile (km)
metrics.definition: Average transportation cost per delivered shipment, or per distance unit moved.
metrics.formula: Per shipment: SUM(cost) / COUNT(DISTINCT shipment_id). Per mile: SUM(cost) / NULLIF(SUM(distance), 0).
metrics.grain: Sum cost and the denominator (shipments or distance) at the SAME grain before dividing. For cost-per-mile, SUM both cost and distance then divide — never AVG(cost_per_mile) per trip (that is distance-unweighted and lets short trips dominate).
anti_patterns: AVG(row_cost_per_mile) instead of SUM(cost)/SUM(distance) — unweighted, so a 2-mile trip skews the network rate.
Joining cost (one row per shipment) to legs/stops (many rows) before summing duplicates cost across legs → inflated total.
Mixing accessorial/fuel-surcharge lines into a per-mile linehaul rate without aligning scope.
metrics.sane_range: currency per shipment or per mile/km, strictly positive. Sub-cent or scientific-notation values ⇒ a units/grain mismatch (e.g. cost summed once, distance fanned out).

metrics.name: Vehicle / Asset Capacity Utilization (Load Factor)
metrics.definition: How full vehicles/containers are — used capacity against available capacity (by weight, volume/cube, or pallet positions).
metrics.grain: Trip/vehicle grain, but CAPACITY-WEIGHTED: sum used and available capacity across trips, then divide. Do NOT AVG(per-trip ratio) — a near-empty van and a full linehaul truck would count equally. Pick ONE basis (weight OR cube OR pallets) and keep it consistent; the binding constraint is often cube, not weight.
anti_patterns: AVG(used/available) per trip instead of SUM(used)/SUM(available) — unweighted, ignores that trips carry different capacities.
Mixing weight-based and volume-based utilization in one number; a truck can be 100% cube-full at 60% weight.
A utilization >1 means used exceeds rated capacity — a data error or a fan-out (capacity joined per-line), not an overloaded truck.
metrics.sane_range: ratio 0..1 (occasionally slightly >1 on legitimate overload, otherwise a bug). Healthy linehaul often 0.7–0.95.
needs_concepts: loaded weight or volume per trip

metrics.name: First-Attempt Delivery Success Rate
metrics.definition: Share of deliveries successfully completed on the first delivery attempt (no redelivery/failed-attempt).
metrics.formula: COUNT(DISTINCT shipment_id WHERE first_attempt_outcome = 'delivered') / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT attempted_shipment_id), 0)
metrics.grain: Shipment grain. A shipment can have MANY attempt rows; collapse to per-shipment first — was the EARLIEST attempt (MIN attempt_time / attempt_seq = 1) successful? Denominator = shipments that had at least one attempt, not total attempt rows.
anti_patterns: COUNT(successful_attempts) / COUNT(all_attempts) measures attempt-level success, not first-attempt-per-shipment — redelivered parcels that eventually succeed inflate it.
Not pinning to the FIRST attempt (use attempt sequence or earliest timestamp); any-attempt success ≠ first-attempt success.
Counting attempt rows in the denominator fans out shipments with multiple attempts.

metrics.name: Dwell Time
metrics.definition: Time a vehicle/driver/shipment spends idle at a facility (dock, yard, port, terminal) between arrival and departure.
metrics.formula: SUM(departure_time - arrival_time) / COUNT(DISTINCT stop_id)  -- average per stop/visit
metrics.grain: Stop/visit grain (one arrival→departure interval per facility visit). Aggregate at the level you report (per facility, per carrier). Each interval is independent; do not sum overlapping intervals or count a vehicle's multiple visits as one.
anti_patterns: Averaging dwell across shipments when many shipments share ONE vehicle stop double-counts the same dwell interval — measure at the stop, not the shipment.
Using order/created time as arrival; dwell is gate-in to gate-out (or arrival to departure), not order-to-departure.
Negative or zero dwell ⇒ swapped/duplicate arrival-departure timestamps; filter and surface, don't average them in.
metrics.sane_range: minutes to hours, non-negative; cross-dock minutes, port detention hours-to-days. Negative ⇒ timestamp error.

metrics.name: Damage / Exception / Loss Rate
metrics.definition: Share of shipments (or units) flagged as damaged, lost, or exception (mis-delivery, shortage, claim).
metrics.formula: COUNT(DISTINCT shipment_id WHERE has_exception = TRUE) / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT shipped_shipment_id), 0)
metrics.grain: Shipment (or unit) grain — choose one and keep numerator and denominator on the SAME population. A shipment with multiple exception/claim rows counts ONCE; use DISTINCT shipment_id, not COUNT of exception records.
anti_patterns: COUNT(exception_rows) / COUNT(shipments): one shipment with 3 exception lines inflates the numerator → rate can exceed 1.
Denominator = delivered shipments while numerator includes lost (never-delivered) shipments — mismatched populations; denominator should be all SHIPPED.
Mixing unit-level damage (numerator) with shipment-level totals (denominator) without aligning grain.
metrics.sane_range: ratio 0..1, typically very small (0.001–0.05); a value near 1 or >1 signals row fan-out.

metrics.name: Deliveries per Route / Driver
metrics.definition: Average number of successful deliveries (stops) completed per route or per driver per period.
metrics.formula: COUNT(DISTINCT delivered_stop_id) / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT route_id), 0)  -- or per driver_id per day
metrics.grain: Route or driver-day grain. Aggregate deliveries to the route/driver-day FIRST, then average — pin the denominator to a clear unit (a route, or a driver+date), otherwise a driver running 3 routes a day skews the ratio. Count DISTINCT delivered stops, not delivery-event/scan rows.
anti_patterns: COUNT(deliveries) / COUNT(drivers) without a time dimension conflates a busy day with a slow one — fix the period (per driver-day).
Counting scan/tracking events instead of distinct delivered stops fans out the numerator.
Including failed/attempted-only stops in 'deliveries' overstates productivity; count completed deliveries.
metrics.sane_range: count, strictly positive; urban last-mile routes often 80–200 stops/route/day.
